situated immediately adjacent to the Glendora country CIub (rrthe
club"). 
The pending decision involves upgrading the designation
of this parcel from conmercial to high dens j,ty residential.
The clendora country club is a nonprofit corporation which
owns and operates a private golf course and clubhouse facility.
The club has approxinately 488 menbers. Members of the club olrn
an equity interest in the club, the present value of which is
approximately 535,000 per menbership. Members of the club nay
setl their memberships so long as the prospective buyers are
approved by the club, Councilmernber Kuhn and Cornmissioners ode1I,
colby and Bauer are members of the club.
You are the city nanager for the city of Glendora. In that
capacity, and with the authorization of Councilnenber Kuhn and
Plinning Courmissioners odell, colby and Bauer, you seek our advice
to determine whether the conf l ict-of- interest provisions of the
Act v/ouId require these public officials to disqualify thenselveE
fron participating in the proposed decisions.
ANALYSI S
Section 87100 prohibits public officials 
frorn naking,
participating in, or using their official 
position to lnfLuence a
governnental decision in which they know or have reason to know
fhey have a financial interest. 
councilmembers and planning
commissioners are public officials. 
(Section 82048.)
An official 
has a financial interest in a decision within the
rneaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable - that the
decision will have a rnaterial financial effect, distinguishable
frorn its effect on the public generally, on the offj-cia1,' or on a
lnemuer of the officiaL'i 
inmediate famity,3 or on:
(a) Any business entity in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars (S1,000) or ntore.
(b) Any real property in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.
(c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other thin loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terrns available to the public without regard to
official 
status, aggregating two hundred fifty
3  An official′ s l'immediate famlly:t are his or her spouse and
dependent chi■dren.  (SeCtion 82029。
)

dollars (5250) or more in value provided to,
received by or prornised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the tine when the
decision is nade.
(d) Any busj.ness entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of rnanagement '
(e) Any donor of, or any internediary or
agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating
trro hundred fifty dollars ($250) or nore in value
provided to, recei.ved by, or pronised to the public
official within 12 nonths prlor to the tine when
the decision is made.
For purposes of this section, indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest olrned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official, by an agent on behalf of a
public official, or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official's agents,
spouse, and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneflcially a lo-percent interest
or greater.
Section 87103.
Makinq, Participatino in uakinq, or AttenDtinq to Influence a
Governmental Decision
A public official makes a governnental decision or
participates in the making of a governnental decision lrhenever the
public official votes on a matter, co:units the agency to a course
6f action, or enters into any contractual agreenent on behalf of
the agency. (Regulation 18700(b). Additionally, a public.
official particilates in a governrnental decision when, actingl
within thi authoiity of his or her position, the public official:
(1) Negotiates, l.rithout significant
substantive ievier.r, with a governnental entity or
private person regarding the decision,' or
(2') Advises or nakes reconrnendations to the
decision-maker, either directly or without
significant intervening substantive review, by:
(A) Conducting research or rnaking any
inves€ifation which requires the exercise of
judgmen€ on the part of the official or designated
6rnpioy"" and the purpose of which is to influence
the decisioni or

(B) Preparing or presenting any report, .
analysis or opinion, orally or in writing, which
requires the exercise of judgment on the part of
the official or desi.gnated employee and the purpose
of which is to influence the decision.
Regulation 18700 (c) .
with regard to a governmental decision which is
within or before an official's agency or an aqency
appointed by or subject to the budqetary control of his
oi^h.. agenly, an oificial is atternpting to use his or
her official- position to influence the decision if, for
the purpose of influencinq the decision, the official
contlctl, or aPpears before, or otherwise attenPts to
influence any lnenber, officer, ernployee or consultant of
the agency. Attempts to influence include, but are not
linitia t-o, appearinces or contacts by the official on
behalf of a business entity, client, or customer.
(Regulation 1.8700. 1. )
Accordingly, if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the financial interests of councilnember Kuhn and
Planning Comnissioners odelI, colby and Bauer t.rill be
rnateriaily affected by a decision, they nust not only
disoualify thenselves fron participating in forrnal
gorr6rrr.ental decisions vhich may affect-such interests,
6ut they rnust also abstain fron attenpting to influence
such delisions by conmunicating with other nembers of
the council, the planning commission, or the staff
regarding the decisions.
You have requested our advice to deterrnine whether
rnernbership in the Glendora country Club constitutes an
interest in real property for purposes of the Act' You
have advised rne tirat- the club itself, a nonprofit
organization, hotds title to the land. Moreover, the
vaiue of the rnembership is not related to increases or
decreases in the assets of the club or the value of the
Iand upon which it is located. Based upon these facts,
,e contlude that the public officials on $,hose behalf
you request our advisl do not have an interest in real
irop"r€y by virtue of their membership in the club'
Holrever, their roenbership in the club, which is
transferable for its fair market value, is an asset'
tvickers Advice Letter, No. I-89-575.) Accordingly,

pirticipating in governmental decisions regarding the.

Foreseeabi Ii.tv
The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. To be
foreseeable, the effect of a decision must be rnore than a mere
possibility,' however, certainty is not required. (Dor.rnev cares v.
bownev Communitv Development Comrn' (1987) 195 cal.APp.3d 983, 989-
991; witt v. l,lorro!, (1977) 7O Ca1.App.3d 817; f!--rClho@. 
(1975)
1 FPPC ops. 198. The Act seeks to prevent more than actual
conflicts of interest; it seeks to prevent even the appearance of
a possible conflict of interest. (witt v. U.9EE9ts, supra at 823.)
one of the parcels which wi]l be the subiect of the pending
decisions is located immediately adjacent to the c1ub. lloreover,
although the club is not situated in the city's redevelopnent

pending decisions r.rilI result in an upgrade in the designation of

It would appear that it is reasonably foreseeable that
decisions regarding this parcel would have a financial effect on a
membership in the Glendora country cIub. It ls foreseeable that
expanded ind improved facilities offering nore desirable. services
woirld result in- an increase in the value of the :nernbership.
ttaintaining the current cornmercial use designatlon of the Parcel
immediatelf adjacent to the club rather than upgrading it to high
density resiaeitiat rnight rnake the parcel rnore affordable if the
club were to determine to purchase the parcel for purposes of
expansion. Thus, it is foreseeable that any potential. expansion
unl irp.o.renent of club facilities would result in an lncrease in
the vaiue of the mernbership. It is also foreseeable that land
uses in the inmediate vicinity of the club which are incornpatible
with the types of activities provided by the club would resuLt in
a decline iir tf,e value of club nernbership. For exanple, if land
surrounding the country club were designated for less desirable
uses such is the siting of a solid waste transfer station or for a
sewage treatment plant, it is foreseeable that menbership ln the
club would becone less desirable. This is so because the use and
enjoyment of club activities would decline. The value of the
rneirUlrstrip $rould thus decline. Accordingly, it-is reasonably
foreseeabie that decisions regarding the lot adjacent to the club
witl have an effect on the value of club menbership. If the
effect wilI be rnaterial, disqual ification lrouId be required'

Materialitv
Regulation 18702 sets forth the guidelines for determining
r,rhether an official's 
economj.c interest in a decision is
uraterially affected by a decision. If the official's 
economic
interest is directlv involved in the decision, Regulation la702.1
provides the appropriate standard for assessing rnateriality.
However, when the official's financial interests will be affected
indirectly by a decision, the appropriate standards for
deterrnining nateriality are those of Regulations 18702.2 through
7A702.6. When an official's interests in reat property are
indirectlv involved in a governnental decision, the appropriate
standard for determining nateriality is that of Regulation
]-8702.3. These regu).ations are discussed below.
As stated above, these officials have a personal asset in the
forn of club tnenbership but they do not have an interest in real
property due to their nembership in the club, Regulation LA?OZ.L
provides that the effect of a decision is rnaterial if the decision
iri1l result in the personal, expenses, incone, assets (other than
interest in real property), or liabilities of the official or his
or her innedlate fanily increasing or decreasing by at least $250.
Therefore, in order to deterurine whether these public officials
who hold nenbership in the club nust disgualify thenselves fron
participating in decisions regarding the lot Lrhich is located
adjacent to the club, you must deternj.ne whether the decisions
will affect the value of the rnenbership in the club by at least
S250.
PubIic qenera I Iv
Even if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
decision is naterial, disqua lification is required only if the
effect is distinguishable fron the effect on the public generally.
(Section 87100.) If the decision does not affect atl nembers of
the public in the same nanner, disqual if ication nay be required
unless the effect of the decision on the public official,s
financial interests is the same as the effect on a significant
segment of the public. (Regulation 18703.) For purposes of our
discussion, the public is the entire population of the city. 
(I-D
re Leoan (1985) 9 FPPC Ops. 1,' In re Olren (L976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77;
Joroensen Advice Letter, No. A-90-017. )
You have advised us that the club has approximately 488
nenbers. The total population of the city of Glendora is
approxirnately 48,000, Thus, tnernbers of the club constitute
approxinately one percent of the total population of the city.
Such a sma1l percentage is insufficient for the 'rpublic aenerally"
exception to apply.

iitnin the jurisdiction, we are unable to deternine whether

qovernmental decisions which are segmented frorn the decisions in
ritich tne official has a financial interest.
we have previously advised that under certain circunstances,
large and comlIex decisions may be divided into separate decisions
whei an official has a disqualifying interest in one cornponent of
